posts.title: How Vector Databases Power RAG
posts.subtitle: Turning meaning into geometry so LLMs can retrieve facts
posts.summary: Vector databases store embeddings and use approximate nearest-neighbor search to fetch the most relevant chunks for a query — the retrieval half of RAG.

slides.title: Vector Databases
slides.body: The retrieval engine behind Retrieval-Augmented Generation.

slides.title: LLMs forget
slides.body: Models can't see your private docs and have a fixed knowledge cutoff.

slides.title: Embed, then search
slides.body: Convert text to vectors that capture meaning, then find the closest ones to the query.

slides.title: The RAG loop
bullets: Chunks are stuffed into the prompt

questions.prompt: What does a vector database actually store?
questions.explanation: It stores embeddings plus metadata for nearest-neighbor retrieval.

questions.prompt: Why use ANN instead of exact search?
options: It scales to millions of vectors fast
questions.explanation: Approximate nearest-neighbor trades a little accuracy for large speed gains.

references.title: Pinecone: What is a vector database?

posts.title: The CAP Theorem, Intuitively
posts.subtitle: Why distributed systems can't have it all
posts.summary: Under a network partition, a distributed system must choose between consistency and availability — you can't guarantee both.

slides.title: CAP Theorem
slides.body: Pick two: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance.

slides.title: The three letters
bullets: Consistency: every read sees the latest write
Availability: every request gets a response

slides.title: Partitions aren't optional
slides.body: Networks fail, so P is mandatory. The real choice is C vs A during a partition.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: CAP is about behavior during partitions
CP refuses to serve stale data
AP stays up and reconciles later

questions.prompt: During a partition, CAP forces a choice between…
questions.explanation: P is a given on real networks, so you trade C against A.

posts.title: Docker Images vs Containers
posts.subtitle: The blueprint and the running house
posts.summary: An image is an immutable, layered template; a container is a running, writable instance of that image.

slides.title: Layers all the way down
slides.body: Each Dockerfile instruction adds a cached, read-only layer.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: Build an image once, run many containers
Containers add a thin writable layer

posts.title: What Model Context Protocol Solves
posts.subtitle: USB-C for connecting AI to your tools and data
posts.summary: MCP is an open protocol that standardizes how AI apps connect to tools, data, and prompts — one interface instead of N bespoke integrations.

slides.title: Model Context Protocol
slides.body: A universal connector between LLM apps and the outside world.

slides.title: The N×M problem
slides.body: Every app re-implements every integration. It doesn't scale.

slides.title: One protocol
slides.body: Servers expose tools, resources, and prompts. Clients speak one language to all of them.

slides.title: Client ↔ Server
bullets: Host app embeds an MCP client

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: Build a server once, reuse everywhere

questions.prompt: Which is NOT a core MCP primitive?
questions.explanation: MCP exposes tools, resources, and prompts.

posts.title: Database Indexes Without the Mystery
posts.subtitle: Why a B-tree turns a scan into a lookup
posts.summary: An index is a sorted structure that lets the database jump straight to matching rows instead of scanning the whole table.

slides.title: Database Indexes
slides.body: The difference between O(n) and O(log n) at the table level.

slides.title: Full table scans
slides.body: Without an index, WHERE checks every row on disk.

slides.title: The B-tree
slides.body: A balanced, sorted tree lets lookups descend in logarithmic steps.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: Every index has a write cost

questions.prompt: What is the main downside of adding an index?
options: Writes get slower and use more storage
questions.explanation: Indexes must be maintained on every write and consume storage.

posts.title: Kubernetes in One Mental Model
posts.subtitle: Declare desired state; the control loop reconciles
posts.summary: You declare the desired state of your workloads; Kubernetes controllers continuously reconcile reality toward that state.

slides.title: Desired vs actual
slides.body: You write YAML describing what should be true. Controllers make it true.

posts.title: Prompt Engineering That Scales
posts.subtitle: Structure beats clever wording
posts.summary: Reliable prompts are structured: clear role, explicit task, constraints, and examples — not magic phrasing.

slides.title: Prompt Engineering
slides.body: Design the input like an API contract.

slides.title: Few-shot wins
slides.body: Two or three good examples usually beat a paragraph of instructions.

questions.prompt: What most reliably improves output quality?
questions.explanation: Few-shot examples anchor the model's behavior more than wording tricks.

posts.title: Idempotency for Reliable APIs
posts.subtitle: Make retries safe by design
posts.summary: An idempotent endpoint produces the same result no matter how many times an identical request is retried — essential for safe distributed systems.

slides.title: Idempotency
slides.body: Retry without fear of double charges.

slides.title: Networks lie
slides.body: A client can't tell a lost response from a failed request, so it retries.

slides.title: Idempotency keys
slides.body: The client sends a unique key; the server stores the result and replays it on retry.

questions.prompt: How do you make a POST safe to retry?
questions.explanation: An idempotency key lets the server dedupe repeated identical requests.
